title: Proposal
text:
Does exposure to natural disasters during impressionable years (high school age) shape the long-term environmental preferences of U.S. legislators?
This project studies whether early-life disaster exposure influences:

The environmental sentiment of legislators’ speech in the Congressional Record
Their revealed environmental policy stance (LCV score)




Legislators who experienced more severe disaster exposure during high school years use more pro-environment language in congressional speech.
Legislators with higher disaster exposure during high school years have higher LCV scores.
Effects are stronger for exposure occurring during impressionable ages than for non-impressionable periods.

title: Proposal
text:
Legislators who experienced more severe disaster exposure during high school years use more pro-environment language in congressional speech.
Legislators with higher disaster exposure during high school years have higher LCV scores.
Effects are stronger for exposure occurring during impressionable ages than for non-impressionable periods.

title: Proposal
text:
Method
This study combines individual-level panel construction with text-based sentiment outcomes and policy score outcomes, linking early-life county exposure to later political behavior.
Wild bootstrap standard errors will be used to account for clustering at the county level in disaster exposure.(\(\ref{@falco_natural_2023}\))

Robustness Checks(tentative ideas)

I will reassign the treatment(exposure to disasters) that occurred during the university period and compare the results. Alternatively, identify a time period during which the politician’s location can be determined, and assign the treatment to that period.
Placebo test for differnent roll call voting outcomes that are not related to the environment, such as economic policy votes. (\(\ref{@}\))

title: Proposal
text:
Sample
Politicians who was born after 1946 because disaster data is available from 1953 and high school years are typically between ages 14-18. The total number of legislators with high school description on official bioguide is 885 out of 1335 (0.663). The final sample size will depend on the success of matching high school location within the US to disaster data, but I expect to have at least 800 legislators in the final analysis.

title: Proposal
text:
Dependent Variables

Congressional speech sentiment (1971-2023) (Progress) Source: USCR Congressional Record API and related text corpora

https://data.stanford.edu/congress_text

https://github.com/unitedstates/congressional-record/

LCV score (1971-2023) (Done) Source: League of Conservation Voters scorecards and Harvard Dataverse

https://scorecard.lcv.org/

https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/GCGJJA

ClimateBERT model Source: ClimateBert: A Pretrained Language Model for Climate-Related Text. (2022) https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.12010 https://www.chatclimate.ai/climatebert


Independent Variable

Disaster exposure during high school years (Progress) Source:

FEMA Disaster Declarations Summary (county-level, 1953-present) https://www.fema.gov/about/openfema/data-sets
Biography data https://bioguide.congress.gov/
High school location(County-level) https://nces.ed.gov/programs/edge/geographic/schoollocations


Scrape biographical text to identify high school location and match to county-level disaster data


Controls

Party affiliation (Democrat / Republican / Independent)
State-level economic indicators (for example, unemployment rate, GDP per capita)
Legislator demographics (for example, age, gender, education)
Additional political controls from Harvard Dataverse

https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/R1PCY6

title: Proposal
text:
May

Finalize data collection.
Complete high school-to-county matching.
Finalize disaster exposure construction.

title: Proposal
text:
June

Conduct manual annotation of environmental text for sentiment model training.
Finalize sentiment outcomes from text data.
Build the panel dataset combining sentiment and LCV outcomes.

title: Proposal
text:
July

Run baseline estimations.
Run robustness checks.
Expand manual annotation if model performance is insufficient.
Submit a full draft to the advisor for feedback.

title: Proposal
text:
August

Revise based on feedback.
Finalize and submit the paper.

title: Proposal
text:
References

Falco, C., & Corbi, R. (2023). Natural disasters and preferences for the environment: Evidence from the impressionable years. Economics Letters.
Elliott, R. J. R., et al. (2023). Climate-related natural disasters and voting behavior: Evidence from environmental legislation in the U.S. Senate. Journal of the Association of Environmental and Resource Economists.

title: Short Summary
text: Do U.S. senators strategically adjust the environmental speech in response to electoral incentives, and does this effect differ by party affiliation?

title: Short Summary
text: Do U.S. senators strategically adjust the environmental speech in response to electoral incentives, and does this effect differ by party affiliation?

title: Short Summary
text:
Motivation
While a robust literature establishes that the staggered structure of the U.S. Senate creates electoral incentives that drive legislators to strategically shift their voting behavior as re-election nears, relying solely on binary roll-call records provides a limited view of representation (Bouton et al. (2021), Ansolabehere, Snyder, and Stewart (2001), Bohr (2025)). Such records capture final legislative decisions but fail to reflect the nuanced intensity of political engagement and the strategic framing of issues that occur in the public eye (Ansolabehere, Snyder, and Stewart (2001), Bohr (2025)). By examining environmental speech, this study uncovers how senators strategically allocate attention and calibrate their speech to navigate the trade-offs between constituent interests and party ideology.

title: Annotation Guideline
text:
Environmental Policy Stance Classification Congressional Floor Speeches — 108th–112th Congress (2003–2012)



Annotation proceeds in two steps:
Step 1 — Relevance check: Is this segment about one of the six policy categories? Step 2 — Stance classification: If yes, assign one of three labels.








Label
Short Definition
Core Criterion




POSITIVE
Pro-environment
The speaker supports environmental regulation or clean energy policy, promotes renewable energy, or accepts climate/pollution science as real and worrying.


NEGATIVE
Anti-environment
The speaker opposes environmental regulation, promotes fossil fuel development or continuation, or questions, denies, or minimizes the scientific evidence on climate change or pollution.


NEUTRAL
No clear stance
The speaker describes policy content, reports facts, or takes no discernible position.



Labels are assigned on the basis of speech content alone. Two independent axes determine the label:

Policy stance — does the speaker support or oppose environmental regulation? Does the speaker promote clean energy or fossil fuel development?
Science stance — does the speaker treat climate change or pollution as established fact and express concern, or cast doubt on the science?

Either axis is sufficient to assign POSITIVE or NEGATIVE. The labels reflect what the speaker says, not a moral judgment about environmentalism.




The texts are speech segments extracted from the U.S. Congressional Record. Each segment consists of a keyword-matched target sentence plus up to three sentences before and after it (a 7-sentence window). Segments are often incomplete and may be cut off mid-argument.
The annotation covers six policy categories selected for their salience in congressional debate and their clear potential for POSITIVE/NEGATIVE stance expression:







Category
Core Content




Clean Air
Air pollution; Clean Air Act; EPA rules; emission standards; tailpipe limits; CAFE standards


Clean Energy
Renewable energy; energy efficiency; electric vehicles; clean electricity


Clean Water
Water quality; Clean Water Act; coal ash; mine drainage


Climate Change
Global warming; greenhouse gas; carbon policy; Paris Agreement


Dirty Energy
Fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas); harmful subsidies; tar sands


Wildlife
Endangered Species Act; habitat protection; fisheries; migratory birds



Other environmental topics (Toxics, Public Lands, Agriculture, etc.) are outside scope.







Discusses a specific law, regulation, or agency action (Clean Air Act, EPA rulemakings, cap-and-trade, Clean Power Plan, etc.)
Takes a position — supportive or critical — on an environmental or energy policy
Frames fossil fuels, renewable energy, or emissions in an evaluative or policy context
Promotes or advocates for fossil fuel development, extraction, or infrastructure (coal, oil, natural gas, tar sands, LNG, pipelines, etc.) regardless of the framing used
Uses environmental language to justify or oppose a spending or appropriations decision
Argues economic harm (cost, job loss, price increase) and names an environmental regulation as the cause, or grounds the harm in climate science or environmental degradation
References climate science facts (temperature projections, sea level rise, etc.) with any evaluative framing — concern, urgency, or skepticism
Addresses conservation programs, habitat protection, or wildlife management in a policy context (ESA listings, species recovery, fisheries regulation)





Mentions a keyword incidentally with no policy position (e.g., “natural gas” pricing in an unrelated economic speech)
Is procedural floor speech or an administrative document with no stance on policy
Discusses energy prices, commodity costs, or job losses with no connection to environmental regulation or scientific fact
Concerns a foreign country’s energy or climate policy without connection to U.S. legislation
Concerns topics outside the six categories: nuclear energy/waste, metal mining, food safety, gun regulation, natural disaster response without policy framing
Introduces an individual by describing their background or qualifications without expressing approval of their environmental work — if the speaker actively praises the individual’s contributions to environmental protection or conservation, assign YES and evaluate stance accordingly






Apply only to segments with Relevance: YES.


Assign POSITIVE when the speaker:

Explicitly supports a specific environmental regulation or regulatory agency
Argues that environmental regulations produce health, economic, or ecological benefits
Criticizes efforts to weaken, block, or repeal environmental rules
Calls for stricter or new environmental regulations
Supports or promotes renewable energy (solar, wind, etc.) in an energy or environmental policy context
Supports clean energy policy explicitly linked to climate, pollution, or emission goals
Supports ESA listings, habitat protection, or wildlife recovery programs
Treats climate change, air pollution, or water contamination as established scientific fact and expresses concern or urgency about it




Assign NEGATIVE when the speaker:

Explicitly opposes a specific environmental regulation or regulatory agency
Argues that regulations impose excessive economic costs, raise energy prices, or destroy jobs — when regulation is named as the cause
Supports legislation that weakens, delays, or repeals environmental rules
Frames environmental regulation as a barrier to energy production or economic growth
Promotes fossil fuel development, extraction, or infrastructure (coal mining, oil drilling, gas pipelines, etc.) as a primary energy strategy
Questions, denies, or minimizes climate science — citing scientific uncertainty, attacking the IPCC, framing climate change as unproven, or treating it as a matter of opinion rather than fact
Invokes “energy independence” or “energy security” to justify expanding fossil fuel production or opposing clean energy mandates




Assign NEUTRAL when the speaker:

Describes the procedural content of a bill without expressing approval or disapproval
Reports scientific findings in a purely descriptive manner, with no evaluative tone — evident concern or distress about environmental harm is sufficient for POSITIVE
Discusses regulatory administration or liability in a purely informational manner
Presents both sides without indicating a preference






Congressional rhetoric frequently signals position without explicit evaluation. Common patterns:








Phrase / Frame
Tone
Note




“job-killing regulations” / “regulatory burden / overreach”
NEGATIVE



“regulations raise energy / electricity prices”
NEGATIVE
Only when a regulation is named as cause


“clean coal” (without skepticism)
NEGATIVE
Fossil fuel continuation frame


“clean coal” + CCS as primary ask (rare)
NEUTRAL/POSITIVE
Context-dependent


“all of the above” energy policy
NEGATIVE
Usually fossil fuel continuation; verify context


“scientists disagree” / “global warming is a religion”
NEGATIVE
Climate science denial


“energy independence” → domestic drilling / oppose mandates
NEGATIVE
Follows the proposed remedy, not the phrase


“energy independence” → renewables / efficiency
POSITIVE



“carbon tax” (criticizing it)
NEGATIVE



“carbon tax” (supporting it)
POSITIVE



“protecting future generations”
POSITIVE



ESA as “job-killer” / “barrier to development”
NEGATIVE
Follows the property-rights / extraction frame


“voluntary conservation” (opposing mandates)
NEGATIVE
Opposes regulatory wildlife protection







Segments were extracted using these keyword categories. Keyword presence does not guarantee relevance.







Category
Key terms




Clean Air
clean air act, air pollution, air quality, epa regulation(s), epa rule(s), emission standard(s), ozone, mercury rule/standard, new source review, mact, csapr, cair, tailpipe emission(s), cafe standard(s), vehicle emission(s)


Clean Energy
clean energy, renewable energy, wind/solar energy/power, energy efficiency, fuel economy, clean vehicle(s), vehicle electrification, charging infrastructure, production tax credit


Clean Water
clean water act, water pollution, water quality, safe drinking water, coal ash, mountaintop removal, mine drainage, stream protection


Climate Change
climate change, global warming, greenhouse gas(es), carbon tax, cap and trade, carbon emission(s), carbon pollution, carbon dioxide, endangerment finding, clean power, kyoto protocol, paris accord/agreement


Dirty Energy
fossil fuel(s), coal industry, coal plant(s), coal mine(s), clean coal, offshore drilling, hydraulic fracturing, natural gas, war on coal, tar sands, keystone pipeline


Wildlife
endangered species, species recovery, migratory bird(s), wildlife habitat, biodiversity, coral reef, fisheries


Cross-cutting
environmental regulation(s), environmental policy, environmental protection, epa, ghg, regulatory burden, regulatory relief



Terms like natural gas, clean coal, and pollution control are especially prone to incidental mentions. Your relevance = no codes are as valuable as relevance = yes codes.

title: About
text: About this site

title: Proposal
text:
Environmental Tone in Congressional Speeches 
Congressional floor speeches from the U.S. House of Representatives are classified into three categories, including positive, negative, and neutral for environment protection, based on human-annotated training data and a ClimateBERT-based classifier fine-tuned for this task.
The reference standard for classification is the League of Conservation Voters (LCV) Congressional Scorecard and ****: a speech segment is pro-environment if it advocates positions that the LCV would score favorably (e.g., supporting Clean Air Act regulations, renewable energy investment, or climate action), and anti-environment if it advocates positions the LCV would score unfavorably (e.g., opposing EPA rules, promoting fossil fuel production without environmental caveats, or dismissing climate science). Segments that describe policy without taking a position are coded as neutral.
Annotation is restricted to topics tied to public attention and LCV voting: Clean Air, Clean Energy, Clean Water, Climate Change, Wildlife,and Dirty Energy. Other LCV categories (e.g., Toxics, Public Lands) are out of scope. For the detailed annotation criteria, see the Annotation Guideline.
The outcome variable is the tone score: the share of positive speeches minus the share of negative speeches among all environment-related speeches per member per Congress, following Card et al. (2022).
\[\text{Tone Score}_{i,t} =
\frac{\text{Positive}_{i,t} - \text{Negative}_{i,t}}
{\text{Positive}_{i,t} + \text{Negative}_{i,t} + \text{Neutral}_{i,t}}\]
The score ranges from \(-1\) (all speeches anti-environment) to \(+1\) (all speeches pro-environment). It is designed to be comparable to the LCV score in direction: members with higher LCV scores are expected to have higher tone scores.
Source: Congressional Record parsed via Hein-Bound corpora (gentzkow_congressional_nodate?)

https://data.stanford.edu/congress_text

https://github.com/unitedstates/congressional-record/

1.1 ClimateBERT Classifier
Speech segments are classified using ClimateBERT (webersinke_climatebert_2022?), a domain-specific language model pretrained on climate-related text. The classification pipeline follows Card et al. (2022) in two stages: (1) a relevance classifier identifies whether a segment discusses one of the five LCV categories; (2) a tone classifier assigns pro-environment, anti-environment, or neutral labels to relevant segments. Both classifiers are fine-tuned on human annotations collected under the guideline above.
Source: ClimateBERT: A Pretrained Language Model for Climate-Related Text (webersinke_climatebert_2022?)

https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.12010

https://www.chatclimate.ai/climatebert

\[\text{Tone Score}_{i,t} =

title: Proposal
text:
Do U.S. senators strategically adjust the environmental speech in response to electoral incentives, and does this effect differ by party affiliation?


While a robust literature establishes that the staggered structure of the U.S. Senate creates electoral incentives that drive legislators to strategically shift their voting behavior as re-election nears, relying solely on binary roll-call records provides a limited view of representationBohr (2025). Such records capture final legislative decisions but fail to reflect the nuanced intensity of political engagement and the strategic framing of issues that occur in the public eyeBohr (2025). By examining environmental speech, this study uncovers how senators strategically allocate attention and calibrate their rhetoric to navigate the trade-offs between constituent interests and party ideology



U.S. Senate terms last six years, with one-third of the seats coming up for election every two yearsBouton et al. (2021). By leveraging this staggered structure, a quasi-experimental design can be implemented (Bouton et al. 2021; Levitt 1996): for any given issue, the assignment of senators to an imminent election group is institutionally predetermined and exogenous to the timing of the debate. This enables a clean comparison of how re-election pressures shape their rhetorical behavior on environmental issues(Bouton et al. 2021).
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tone}_{ijt} ={}& \lambda_0
  + \lambda_1 \left( \text{ElectionYear}_{it} \times \text{Democrat}_{it} \right)
  + \lambda_2 \left( \text{NonElectionYear}_{it} \times \text{Republican}_{it} \right) \\
  &+ \lambda_3 \left( \text{ElectionYear}_{it} \times \text{Republican}_{it} \right)
  + \lambda_4 X_{it}
  + \lambda_5 W_{jt}
  + \delta_j + \delta_t
  + \varepsilon_{ijt}
\end{aligned}
\tag{1}\]
where \(\text{Tone}_{ijt}\) is the sentiment tone of environmental speech by senator \(i\) from state \(j\) in year \(t\). \(\text{ElectionYear}_{it}\) is a dummy equal to 1 in the year the senator faces reelection, and \(\text{NonElectionYear}_{it}\) is its complement. The omitted baseline category is non-election-year Democrats, following Bouton et al. (2021). \(X_{it}\) is a vector of legislator characteristics (e.g., gender, age), \(W_{jt}\) is a vector of state-level controls (e.g., income, education), \(\delta_j\) denotes state fixed effects, and \(\delta_t\) denotes year fixed effects. The key coefficients of interest are \(\lambda_1\) and \(\lambda_3\), which capture how election proximity shifts the tone of environmental speech for Democrats and Republicans, respectively, relative to the baseline of non-election-year Democrats.







Legislators who
Legislators with higher disaster exposure during high school years have higher LCV scores.
Effects are stronger for exposure occurring during impressionable ages than for non-impressionable periods.

title: Proposal
text: While a robust literature establishes that the staggered structure of the U.S. Senate creates electoral incentives that drive legislators to strategically shift their voting behavior as re-election nears, relying solely on binary roll-call records provides a limited view of representationBohr (2025). Such records capture final legislative decisions but fail to reflect the nuanced intensity of political engagement and the strategic framing of issues that occur in the public eyeBohr (2025). By examining environmental speech, this study uncovers how senators strategically allocate attention and calibrate their rhetoric to navigate the trade-offs between constituent interests and party ideology

Legislators with higher disaster exposure during high school years have higher LCV scores.
Effects are stronger for exposure occurring during impressionable ages than for non-impressionable periods.
